text: The camera is introduced through a trocar below the umbilicus, revealing multiple adhesions. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down omental adhesions from the peritoneum and reaches the small bowel loops. Blunt dissection and laparoscopic scissors are used to carefully dissect small bowel adhesions from the peritoneum. After complete adhesiolysis, the small bowel is inspected from the ligament of Treitz to the ileum, where more adhesions are found and removed. The neoterminal ileum with a fat-creeping pattern is reached, and lateral mobilization of the ileum and ascending colon is performed. A mini-laparotomy is made by partially excising the scar of a former midline incision, allowing the mobilized bowel to be extracted and examined. The colon is transected, including an inflammatory polyp, followed by transection of the ileum proximal to the disease. The specimen shows a thickened mesentery and bowel wall. A hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis with interrupted sutures is performed before returning the bowel to the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon inspects an inflammatory mass with an adherent small bowel loop in the right lower quadrant, noting the attachment of the omentum. The small bowel is thoroughly checked for additional Crohn's lesions. The inflammatory mass involves both small and large bowel, with a loop of non-diseased ileum hanging towards the pelvis. The primarily diseased terminal ileum is identified on the left side, fistulizing to a longer segment of secondarily involved ileum. Lateral mobilization begins with traction and countertraction using the harmonic scalpel, ensuring the right ureter is checked to prevent injury. The mobilization proceeds cephalad towards the hepatic flexure, and the view shifts to the gallbladder and liver. The mobile mass is exteriorized through a peri-umbilical incision, with the attached loop of ileum resected first, followed by the main lesion through ileo-colectomy and end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon begins by running the small bowel distally from the ligament of Treitz using two bowel clamps. The ileum disappears beneath an inflammatory tumor with a smooth surface, and adherent epiploic appendices from the neighboring sigmoid colon are removed. Lateral mobilization starts at the ascending colon, moving proximally towards the ileum, using electrocautery for tissue separation. Traction and countertraction are applied to aid dissection. The inflammatory mass with a sealed perforation towards the iliac fossa is exposed, and dense adhesions are carefully dissected. Mobilization continues along the ascending colon and hepatic flexure until reaching the omentum attachments. A mini laparotomy is performed above and below the umbilicus to exteriorize the bowel loops. Electrocautery is used to transect the bowel on both sides, and the specimen is removed, followed by an end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for sharp and blunt dissection to mobilize the bowel from lateral to medial. The colon is transected above the cecum, and the ileum is palpated to locate the capsule camera. A longitudinal incision is made on the bowel wall opposite the mesentery to retrieve the capsule. The final view shows the hand-sewn end-to-end ileocolostomy.
